title: Working with the Part That Wants to Disappear
researcherTopic: observation without the observer, freedom from conditioning
metaDescription: Explore why a part of you learned to disappear as a survival mechanism and how to gently invite it back into presence. Learn to work with dissociation and C-PTSD.
imageDescription: A cracked ceramic bowl is being mended with gold, the light catching the delicate repairs. The hands holding the bowl are gentle and steady, suggesting a process of patient, beautiful healing. The background is soft and out of focus, filled with warm morning light.
finalH2: Freedom in the Face of Constraint
htmlContent:
Jiddu Krishnamurti, the philosopher and speaker, dedicated his life to the inquiry of a single, genuine question: can the mind observe its own workings without the observer, without the entity that judges, fragments, and ultimately separates itself from the very reality it is attempting to perceive? This question is not some abstract philosophical koan, but the very heart of the work we must do when we find ourselves contending with a part of our own consciousness that has learned, as a matter of survival, to simply disappear. We might feel a strange sense of shame or confusion around this internal vanishing act, but the nervous system is not concerned with our stories about it; it is simply running a program that was, at one point, deeply intelligent.

The Architecture of Internal Vanishing

The impulse to disappear is not a character flaw or a sign of weakness; it is a highly sophisticated survival strategy, an architecture of internal vanishing built brick by brick by a nervous system that correctly assessed its environment as unsafe for full presence. For many who have navigated the terrain of complex trauma, the world did not present itself as a place that could handle the full spectrum of their being... their anger was too much, their needs were a burden, their very existence an inconvenience. In my years of working in this territory, I've seen this outcome as a sudden fogginess in the middle of a conversation, a complete memory blank where a painful event should be, or a pervasive sense of being a spectator to one's own life. This is the work of a brilliant protector, a part that learned to press the eject button on reality long before the conscious mind could even register the threat.

We spend so much time trying to reason with this part, but we are speaking the wrong language, using the logic of the prefrontal cortex to negotiate with the primal wisdom of the brainstem. This part doesn’t speak in words; it speaks in sensation, impulse, and the felt sense of threat or safety. The work, then, is not to force this part to emerge, but to create the conditions of safety that might invite it. It is a process of becoming a student of our own nervous system, learning to read its signals not as pathologies to be fixed, but as communications to be understood, meeting the impulse to disappear with a gentle, unwavering curiosity.

The Observer and the Observed

The teaching of Krishnamurti on observation without an observer offers us a powerful lens through which to understand this work. He pointed out that the moment we create a separation between the “I” who is observing and the “thing” being observed... in this case, the part that wants to disappear - we are already in a state of conflict and fragmentation. The “I” becomes the manager, the fixer, the one who is trying to control or eliminate the “unwanted” part. The invitation, then, is to experiment with a different quality of attention, one that does not create this division. Can we observe the impulse to disappear without the observer? This means not observing it *from* a place of judgment or fear, but simply noticing the entire field of experience as it is. Noticing the thought, “I am disappearing,” the sensation of numbness in the limbs, the subtle shift in the breath, all without a central headquarters directing the operation.

What we find here is a deeply active practice, requiring a radical commitment to staying with the raw data of our experience before the mind spins a story. We are not analyzing the part that disappears; we are creating a space of unconditional presence where it can simply be. In this space, integration can happen. By staying with these raw sensations, we de-link them from the narrative of being broken, metabolizing the stored survival energy locked in the nervous system. This is not about getting rid of the part, but expanding our capacity to be with it, holding it within a larger field of awareness. For more on the nature of traumatic memory, resources from organizations like the International Society for the Study of Trauma and Dissociation (ISST-D) can be invaluable.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link).

The Body as an Unwilling Archive

Beyond the Tyranny of Thought

The way out is not through more thinking, but through learning to dis-identify from the stream of thought altogether. Thoughts are not facts; they are events in consciousness. The practice is to shift our attention from the content of the thoughts to the awareness that is perceiving them. What emerges is a radical shift from being the protagonist in the drama of our thoughts to being the spacious, silent witness. From this place, we can have a different relationship with the part that disappears, seeing it not as an enemy but as a wounded part of ourselves needing compassion and presence. This is the essence of what it means to practice non-attachment in the context of healing.

The Practice of Gentle Return

If the impulse to disappear is a road away from the present moment, then the practice of healing is a gentle, persistent return. It is not a dramatic, one-time event, but a series of small, incremental movements back toward ourselves, back toward the body, back toward the life that is waiting to be lived. This return is not about forcing ourselves to be present when the system is screaming “no.” It is about honoring the system’s need for safety while simultaneously expanding its capacity for presence. the natural rhythm of moving between a state of activation and a state of resource - and titration, the practice of touching into the difficult material for just a moment and then returning to a place of relative safety. We are not trying to conquer the fear; we are trying to build a larger container for it, a container of compassion, curiosity, and self-regulation. That becomes the slow, unglamorous work of re-wiring a nervous system, a process that happens not in dramatic breakthroughs, but in the quiet, consistent choices we make from moment to moment.

In daily life, this practice means noticing the first subtle cues of the disappearing part and meeting it with a gentle, internal “hello.” It means having a pre-planned menu of resources to anchor you in the present: the feeling of a warm mug, the sound of birds, or naming five blue things in the room. It is having compassion for yourself on the days when the fog is thick, understanding this is not a failure but the echo of an old survival strategy. That becomes the path of building a life worth living, not by eradicating wounded parts, but by integrating them into a larger, more compassionate whole.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link).

Freedom in the Face of Constraint

Ultimately, the work of working with the part that wants to disappear is a road toward a new definition of freedom. We often think of freedom as the absence of any internal constraint, a life free from anxiety, fear, or the impulse to shut down. But for those of us who carry the imprint of complex trauma, this definition of freedom can be a cruel trap, a setup for perpetual failure. It is the ability to be with the impulse to disappear without being consumed by it. It is the ability to have the thought, “I am unsafe,” without that thought dictating our every action. It is the ability to feel the physiological pull toward shutdown and still, in that same moment, feel our feet on the floor and choose to stay connected to the present, however tenuously.

What we find here is the tender, paradoxical truth at the heart of this work: the part of you that learned to disappear holds the key to your most striking and integrated presence. It is by turning toward the absence that we find the ground of our being. And it is by honoring the constraints of our own nervous system that we discover a freedom that is not contingent on circumstance, a freedom that is strong, resilient, and utterly our own.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do I feel so much shame about dissociating or “checking out”?

Shame is an incredibly common response to dissociation because it often feels like a personal failing, a lack of willpower or mental toughness. Society prizes constant presence and productivity, so when our system takes us offline, it can feel like we are at its core flawed. The shame is a secondary layer of suffering, a story the mind tells about a physiological process it doesn’t understand.

For practical self-compassion exercises, a popular workbook is The Self-Compassion Workbook (paid link).

Seriously.

Is it possible to completely get rid of the part that wants to disappear?

The goal is not to eliminate this part, but to integrate it. This part of you is a brilliant protector that helped you survive overwhelming experiences. Over time, its impulse to disappear will lessen as its sense of safety in the present moment grows, but the core part is a piece of your history to be honored, not exiled.

How can I explain this to my partner or loved ones?

Explaining this can be challenging, but using analogies can help. You might describe it as a faulty internal fire alarm that goes off even when there’s no fire, or an internal circuit breaker that trips to prevent the whole system from being overloaded. It can be helpful to direct them to resources on complex trauma, like the work of Stephen Porges, so they can understand the physiological roots of the experience.

A simple supplement that many find supports their mood is Garden of Life Vitamin D3 5000 IU (paid link).

What is one small thing I can do when I feel myself starting to disappear?

Engage your senses in a very simple, non-demanding way. Instead of trying to force yourself back into a complex conversation, shift your attention to a single, neutral sensory input. Run your hands under cool water and notice the temperature. Pop a sour candy in your mouth. These strong, simple sensations can act as an anchor, gently pulling your awareness back to the physical reality of the present moment without overwhelming the system.
